text: The surgeon begins the procedure for a left indirect inguinal hernia by creating space laterally and working anteriorly to take down the peritoneal flap.
The surgeon cauterizes the medial umbilical ligament, with visible adhesion causing the peritoneal flap to appear sticky.
The surgeon exposes the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum, noting a thinner area on the medial side, and then crosses over to connect the tissue planes.
The surgeon carefully thins the peritoneal flap laterally, pushing the fat to cover the nerves, while approaching the deep internal ring.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels positioned superiorly and to the right, indicating an indirect inguinal hernia. The iliopubic tract is visible, running medially from the anterior superior iliac spine to the pubic tubercle.

The surgeon identifies the nerves running along the iliopubic tract beneath a large cord lipoma, which is being taken down. The cord lipoma and hernia sac are being addressed simultaneously due to their close association.
The surgeon works on the medial portion, applying touch cautery to pull down tissue while examining the iliopubic tract to ensure nerves are not being pulled into the specimen.
The surgeon demonstrates the separation of tissue layers, which extends far but separates cleanly, revealing distinct anatomical structures.
The surgeon applies traction and counter-traction, pulling up and laterally, revealing the spermatic vessels and the vas deferens in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the edge of the hernia sac and carefully manipulates it downward, avoiding lateral thinning. The cord lipoma is truncated, allowing fat to lay laterally to prevent nerve involvement. The cord lipoma is then removed.

The surgeon continues to dissect and take down the hernia sac, creating sufficient space in the surgical field. The patient is five foot nine, and an extra-large instrument is being used for the procedure.

The surgeon positions the instruments directly on the pubic bone and Cooper's ligament to ensure precise needle placement, avoiding blind insertion into the bone and potential complications like hitting the corona mortis.
The surgeon carefully manipulates tissue around the iliac vein, maintaining a lateral plane to avoid drifting into adjacent structures.

The surgeon places an extra-large mesh over the indirect inguinal hernia site and begins suturing on the side of the hernia. High lateral fixation is applied to ensure the mesh remains deep within the pocket.

The surgeon assesses the area for high medial fixation but decides against it due to obscuring fat, opting instead for fixation at the Cooper's ligament and high lateral position, ensuring stability of the mesh.
The surgeon performs a standard flap closure to prevent mesh migration, ensuring the mesh remains securely positioned.
